mnemonics: Pigeons Crowded: Too many pigeons (items), too few holes (containers) guarantees a shared space. Remember: Overcrowding always leads to sharing.
keyPoints: The Pigeonhole Principle states that if n+1 or more objects are placed into n boxes, then at least one box contains two or more objects.
It is a fundamental counting argument used to prove the existence of a property without necessarily identifying the specific instance.
It applies to any finite partitioning of a set and is often used in combinatorial proofs and problem-solving.
commonMistakes: Misapplying the principle to infinite sets (it only applies to finite sets).
Forgetting that the principle guarantees existence but does not specify which pigeonhole contains multiple items.
Incorrectly calculating the required number of items (e.g., using n instead of n+1 for the minimal case).
